title: Sex After Injury or Major Health Change
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: Field notes for physical limitations, pain, adaptation, and partnered problem-solving.

content.text: Your body changed. Maybe you had an injury, surgery, or a diagnosis that limits what you can do physically. Sex was a certain way before, and now positions hurt, pain is unpredictable, or you simply can't do what you used to. You're grieving the way sex used to work. You might be avoiding it entirely because you're afraid of pain or failure. Your partner doesn't know how to help—they don't want to hurt you, but they also miss sexual connection. This isn't about willpower or desire. It's about your body having new constraints and needing a new approach.

items: Pain during or after sex: Certain positions, depths, or duration causes pain—during or hours later.
Difficulty with arousal or orgasm: Pain, medication, or changed nerve sensation makes arousal difficult.
Fear of pain limiting exploration: You avoid trying new things because you're afraid it will hurt.
Asymmetrical desire: One partner wants sex more, the other is cautious about pain or limitation.
Difficulty with penetration: Specific positions or types of penetration are no longer possible.
Fatigue making sex impossible: Energy limits mean sex has to happen at specific times or not at all.
Loss of spontaneity: Sex requires planning, positioning, or medical considerations (pain medication timing, etc).
Feeling like a burden: You believe your injury/illness is making sex worse for your partner.

lines: Physical limitations don't mean the end of sexual intimacy. They mean it has to be reimagined, not abandoned.

content.text: Injury or health change creates physical constraints (pain, limited range, reduced sensation) AND psychological ones (fear, grief, shame). Sex requires vulnerability and risk-taking. If your body hurt you during sex last time, your nervous system gets cautious. Add partner confusion (they don't want to hurt you, but they miss sex), and you get avoidance. The longer you avoid, the more that becomes the new normal.

items: Fear of pain limiting exploration: One painful experience creates catastrophic thinking: 'It will always hurt.'
Partner confusion becoming resentment: They don't know what's safe to try, so they stop trying, you feel rejected.
Grief compounding: Loss of the body you had + loss of the sex you had = doubles the grief.
Avoidance escalating: You haven't had sex in months. Partner feels rejected. Talking about it feels harder.
Medical and sexual disconnect: You talk to your doctor about pain, your partner about missing connection, but no one puts them together.
Medication side effects: Some pain meds reduce libido or sexual response, which gets blamed on injury when it's the medication.
Disability grief: Sex was something your pre-injury body could do. This new body requires adaptation, and that feels unfair.

lines: Why this works: reducing total load and increasing predictability lowers baseline arousal and improves regulation.

content.text: You can adapt sex to your new physical reality and rebuild intimacy through collaboration.

items: Get specific medical guidance: Talk to your doctor about safe sexual activity post-injury. Ask: 'What positions are safe? What pain medication timing matters? What should I avoid?'
Identify pain-free zones and positions: Experiment slowly to find what doesn't hurt. Start there and build from what works.
Separate pain from failure: Pain during sex isn't failure. It's information. 'This position causes pain' doesn't mean you can't have sex, it means try a different position.
Rebuild communication with partner: 'I want sex, but I need us to figure out what works now. This is a problem we solve together, not a failure.'
Try non-traditional sexual expression: If penetration is painful, manual stimulation, oral sex, or other forms become primary. Pleasure doesn't have one shape.
Plan for energy/pain management: Schedule sex at times when you're rested and pain medication is working. Predictability reduces anxiety.
Address medication side effects: If libido dropped with a new medication, talk to your doctor about alternatives or timing.
Practice partnered problem-solving: 'What works for you? What works for me? How do we both feel good?' is a different conversation than 'Can you still want me?'
Grieve the loss: Your old sex was real and good. The new version will be different, not lesser. Both can be true.

lines: Field rule: set the bar low enough to do it even on your worst day.

content.text: Stress communication should be clear, brief, and repeatable. Predictable routines protect relationships.

items: Use concrete language: 'This position causes pain. Let's try this one instead.'
Make it collaborative: 'I want sex with you. We need to figure out what works for both of us now.'
Name the grief: 'I miss how sex used to be. I'm also grieving my body. And I still want intimacy with you.'
Offer specific information: Tell them what hurts, what feels good, what you need. Don't make them guess.

content.text: Self-guided tools are a start. Escalate care when symptoms persist, spread, or begin harming safety and function.

items: Pain during sex is ongoing and limiting your sexual frequency significantly.
You've stopped having sex entirely due to fear of pain.
Partner is becoming resentful or considering ending the relationship.
Medication side effects are impacting arousal/pleasure and it's not improving.
Relationship conflict around sex is affecting your overall connection.
Sex therapy (especially with a therapist educated in pain management) can help couples adapt sex to new physical realities.

content.text: Use the worksheet every day for one week. Track trigger, body signal, and reset action. Keep what works and remove what does not.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Sex After Injury or Major Health Change
worksheets.intro: Use this to track the pattern in this chapter and build a practical response you can repeat under load.

question_groups.label: Where did this pattern show up this week?
question_groups.hint: List specific moments at work, at home, and in transition.

question_groups.label: What did it cost you when it took over?
question_groups.hint: Name impact on body, mood, decisions, and relationships.

question_groups.label: What two actions will you run for the next 7 days?
question_groups.hint: Choose actions small enough to use on hard days.

numbered_prompts.hint: What did your body do first?
What exact behavior will you use next time?

worksheets.closing_note: Track this daily for one week, then tighten the plan around what actually worked.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Sex After Injury or Major Health Change - Partner/Team Check-In
worksheets.intro: Use this page for one short practical check-in each week.

question_groups.label: What do others notice first when this pattern is active?
What support helps most and least?
question_groups.hint: List what helps and what escalates the issue.

question_groups.label: What agreement will you both practice this week?
question_groups.hint: One clear action from each side.

numbered_prompts.hint: What phrase means pause and reset?
What specific repair happens after a rupture?

worksheets.closing_note: Keep check-ins short, specific, and repeatable.
